museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: January 2004: The year begins with whispers of new handhelds and the old console generation still producing heavily.
museum.curatorNote: Initial researched draft for January 2004. Regional timing and UK retail context should be checked before verification.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five researched month markers or context notes.

items.title: The year begins with whispers of new handhelds and the old console generation still producing heavily.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight notable releases, led by month-specific anchors where evidence supports them.

items.title: Unreal Tournament 2004
items.body: Arena shooters reach one of their last grand PC retail forms.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for March 2004.

items.title: Far Cry
items.body: Crytek makes PC islands look impossibly bright and expensive.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for March 2004.

items.title: Ninja Gaiden
items.body: Team Ninja gives Xbox a hard, glossy action showcase.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for March 2004.

items.title: Doom 3
items.body: id's horror-lit corridor shooter becomes a graphics-card trial.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for August 2004.

items.title: Burnout 3: Takedown
items.body: Criterion turns crashing into a design philosophy.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for September 2004.

items.title: Fable
items.body: Lionhead's long-promised RPG arrives smaller than myth, but memorable all the same.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for September 2004.

items.title: Rome: Total War
items.body: Creative Assembly makes strategy feel cinematic on the family PC.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for September 2004.

items.title: Grand Theft Auto: San Andreas
items.body: Rockstar makes a state-sized PS2 landmark full of radio, myth and playground quotation.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for late October 2004.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Platform, buying and industry context for the month and its wider year.

items.title: Nintendo DS launches in North America and Japan
items.body: Dual screens and a stylus offer an odd answer to Sony's coming PSP.
items.sourceNote: Nintendo DS references list November 21 and December 2, 2004.

items.title: PSP launches in Japan
items.body: Sony's handheld arrives as a glossy media device and a serious challenge to Nintendo's portable monopoly.
items.sourceNote: PSP references list December 12, 2004 in Japan.

items.title: PS2 Slim arrives
items.body: Sony makes the market leader smaller and cheaper-looking at the moment competitors need it to seem old.
items.sourceNote: 2004 PS2 Slim context should be verified against regional launch sources.

items.title: Steam becomes unavoidable for Half-Life 2
items.body: Valve ties a major PC release to online activation, making the client impossible to ignore.
items.sourceNote: Half-Life 2 and Steam launch context support this reading.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: How the network felt around the edges of play.

items.title: Forums and files
items.body: Halo 2 makes console online play feel social, competitive and immediate through matchmaking and voice.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period reporting and UK access patterns; verify with period web/magazine sources before final publication.

items.title: Services and servers
items.body: World of Warcraft starts turning guilds, subscriptions and online schedules into ordinary gaming language.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period reporting and UK access patterns; verify with period web/magazine sources before final publication.

items.title: Voice and identity
items.body: Steam activation around Half-Life 2 makes PC ownership feel newly account-based.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period reporting and UK access patterns; verify with period web/magazine sources before final publication.

items.title: Everyday connection
items.body: Forums and magazine cover discs still sit beside the network; the old and new delivery systems overlap.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period reporting and UK access patterns; verify with period web/magazine sources before final publication.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A curator's narrative sketch of the month as lived culture.

items.title: The room
items.body: It felt like too much arrived at once. San Andreas, Halo 2, Half-Life 2, WoW, Metal Gear, Burnout and handheld news all seemed to compete for the same oxygen.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.

items.title: The shelf
items.body: The old console generation did not feel old; it felt overpowered with possibility.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.

items.title: The conversation
items.body: The DS and PSP made the future portable before the living-room next generation had even begun.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.

items.title: The afterimage
items.body: By Christmas, online play had changed tone: not a novelty, not universal, but clearly part of where games were going.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.
